title: Page 2
text:
FALL
2025
CONTENTS
Dear Reader,
Welcome to this semester’s issue of “Sharpur Edge”, where we turn the
spotlight onto the creative community of Binghamton University, the School of
the Arts. In these pages, we explore the innovation and imagination that flow
through these departments where our student artists, performers, and creators
bring their visions to life.

In this edition, we see how the School of Arts fosters an environment
where creativity is encouraged and collaboration is celebrated. This issue is
a tribute to the individuals who make those stories possible, capturing the
essence of what it means to be an artist in today’s world.

A special thanks to Harpur Edge’s incredible advisors, Erin Cody and
Megan Konstantakos, for their guidance throughout the creation of this issue.
Their dedication, along with the contributions of our University partners, made
this magazine possible. We hope you enjoy exploring the creativity and talent
that fills these pages!
All the best,
Grace and Rayaan

title: Page 3
text:
Your portfolio is the most important tool
in showcasing your work. Our Google
Drive offers portfolio inspiration that can
be applied to different art forms, whether
that be theater, photography, graphic
design, etc. By following the guides, you
can ensure that your work is presented in
a structured and visually appealing way.
The art of writing a standout resume
and cover letter can be daunting. Our
Google Drive simplifies this with resumes
specifically designed for visual and
performance professionals. Our cover
letter guides help craft a narrative that
ties your art practice to the job you’re
applying for, giving you an edge in a
competitive industry.
1. Portfolio Templates:
Building a Future In The Arts
2. Resume & Cover Letter Guides:
3. Networking Tools:
4. Career Exploration:
Our Google Drive includes career
exploration tools to help you assess
different opportunities in the arts
industry. Whether you’re considering
working in freelance design, performing
on stage, or arts administration, these
resources provide insights into various
career paths that align with your artistic
strengths and passions.
5. Interview Preparation:
The interview preparation guides include
common questions and tips on how to
present your work/portfolio effectively.
These resources help you gain the
confidence you need to succeed in highpressure conversations.
Building a strong online
presence is key to growing
your creative career. We have
provided you with resources
that teach you how to market
yourself, including our Mentor
Match program and faculty
contacts.
Harpur Edge also provides a
comprehensive collection of Google Drive
resources tailored to students across
a variety of majors. Whether you’re an
aspiring artist, an economics major, or
on the pre-health track, we
offer a multitude of readiness
resources to help you navigate
your path with confidence. You
can explore these resources
by visiting our website under
the Intellectual Development
tab, where you’ll find curated
materials to support your
professional journey.
Starting a career in the arts requires more than just creating beautiful work; it’s
about presenting that work in a way that appeals to the workforce. Fortunately,
Harpur Edge has built an Arts Career Resource Drive designed specifically for
majors in the School of the Arts, highlighting essential materials that will help
you focus on your craft while also building a professional career.
By: Grace Scollo
THE ARTS ISSUE 3

title: Page 4
text:
FACULTY
Professor Ariana Gerstein has long been known for pushing
the boundaries of experimental filmmaking. As a professor in
Binghamton University’s cinema department and a recipient of
the 2024 Guggenheim Fellowship, she balances her creative work
with a deep commitment to education. In an interview with Harpur
Edge, Gerstein shared insights about her filmmaking process, her
dedication to teaching, and the landscape of cinema at Binghamton.
For Gerstein, receiving the Guggenheim Fellowship represents both a professional achievement
and a source of support by offering her time for
creative freedom. This autonomy is crucial for
Gerstein, whose filmmaking process is rooted
in techniques like single-frame cinematography
and animation.
“Time is the most important element,” she said.
“First ideas should only be the start. The better
ones grow in complexity and new ideas are revealed through the time-consuming work.”
Gerstein’s journey at Binghamton began as an
undergraduate student. After earning her MFA,
she couldn’t turn down the opportunity to return to our Cinema Department. Reflecting on
the department’s evolution, she highlighted its
expansion beyond traditional filmmaking into
areas like animation, sound design, and installation.
“I think it’s the coolest place around,” she emphasized. “We still have the intense dedication,
but now we have more faculty, more options for
working and thinking about cinema.”

CINEMA
CINEMA
One of Gerstein’s
biggest commitments at
Binghamton has been
helping to establish the
university’s new MFA in
Cinema, a program that bridges
the gap between undergraduate study and
professional development.
Gerstein’s approach to filmmaking has always
been about pushing boundaries, something she
encourages in her students.
“The desire to push boundaries is driven by
curiosity,” she said. “You have to do it because
you love it, because it feels essential to living.”
She also accentuates the importance of
collaboration and taking advantage of the
university environment.
“Your time as a student is so valuable… because
learning is the priority,” she explained. “Go to
university events together, like film screenings,
visiting artists, performances and lectures. If you
take advantage of these things, you will be more
prepared to face the world in front of you with
creativity and resilience.”
By: Grace Scollo
4

title: Page 5
text:
STUDENT
Having earned a double degree in Cinema and Physics from Binghamton University in 2023, Zagham Shah’s drive to understand
the world on different levels has defined his career as an undergraduate.
Zagham’s passion for cinematography is deeply rooted in his upbringing:
“I was brought up in a vibrant and diverse culture that helped shape
how I see the world and appreciate life and its intricacies.” He recounted
spending hours in front of the TV rewatching Bollywood classics—“larger
than life stories of love, adventure, pain, and the like.” He continues, “like
most film students, I eventually picked up a camera and started shooting
my life as I saw it.”
Post-graduation, Zagham says that he’s taking
time to evaluate his career trajectory. He’s considering graduate school for physics or further
study in cinema, but ideally would like to work
at the intersection of both disciplines.
The independence, organization, and unique
artistic voice he’s developed studying cinema at
Binghamton helped prepare him for his current
role as a digital media producer for the SUNY
research foundation in the clean energy industry. He plays a crucial role in supporting the
university’s innovation ecosystem through photo
and video marketing.
Zagham ended with wise advice for students
interested in Cinema or other creative fields:
“Treat your coursework as an extension of your
art, voice, and career as early as possible. The
means by which you create work is less important than simply having created the work. Make a
lot of things, simply create as much as possible
no matter the perceived quality and you’ll find
yourself having learned the skills you always
sought.”
By: Rayaan Lodi
SPOTLIGHT
While at Binghamton, Zagham gained hands-on
cinematography experience inside and outside
of the classroom. He was deeply influenced by
the cinema program’s lauded approach to experimental filmmaking, navigating different themes
and styles through freelance projects and student organizations like the Film and Production
Society. Crucially, much of his work explored
identity and culture.
Zagham highlights two projects during his time
as an undergraduate: Homeland (2023), a documentary in which he utilizes visuals that engage
the viewer’s senses to (re)create memories of
ancestral home; and Gedanken (2022), a short
film about a student who, “after listening to the
ramblings of a philosopher, finds himself momentarily enthralled in what the universe and his
imagination conjure.”
Although his second major may seem unrelated to some, Zagham noted that physics helped
inform the creative process behind his art: “I
enjoyed laboratory experiments that helped me
gain a clearer understanding of the world and its
intricate mechanisms, which directly translated
to how I articulated my perspective of the world
through my art.”
THE ARTS ISSUE 5

title: Page 6
text:
Ready…Set…
For 24 hours straight, 10 student-artists created
impromptu still-life drawings for a $500 first
place prize at the 24 Hour Draw-A-Thon.
By Rayaan Lodi
One participant, Alejandra Fernandez, is a
sophomore majoring in psychology. For her,
this competition allowed her to nurture her
artistic side:
Changing my major, my passion from art to
psychology, was always something interesting,
tricky, and complex. Just being back in an art
studio is really exciting.
”
“

title: Page 7
text:
Whether art is your hobby or passion,
the 24-Hour Draw-A-Thon is just one of
numerous opportunities for students of
all backgrounds to explore the arts at
Binghamton University.
Find more pictures
from the Draw-A-
Thon (and other artrelated events) here:
For participant Paige Licursi, a junior graphic design major, this competition
was a rewarding, hands-on supplement to her arts education. Initially slightly
nervous, she reflected on her experience at the end of the event:
“I’m tired! It was exhausting, but definitely worth it, absolutely a great
experience. I had so much fun.”
Draw!
THE ARTS ISSUE 7

title: Page 8
text:
Journey to the Opera .
Lorena Maggiore, an alumna of Binghamton University, was a music and
English & creative writing double major
during her undergraduate studies. Since
graduating, she has continued her musical journey at the Jacobs School of
Music at Indiana University, where she is
pursuing opera professionally and earning her Master of Music in Voice under
the guidance of Carol Vaness.
“At first, I was so nervous working with
someone with so much experience
and such a wide background,” Lorena shared. “Now I feel like I’ve settled
in, and I’m just so grateful to have the
chance to work with her.”
Lorena’s passion for singing was evident
from an early age. While she initially
started with musical theater, her path
shifted towards opera after participating
in a choir and attending a performance
of Don Giovanni at the Metropolitan Opera House in New York. She was fascinated by its grandness and the power of
the vocalists.
“That was the moment when I knew I
wanted to go into opera,” she recalled.
“Something in my heart was like, ‘this is
what I need to do with my life.’”
Networking also played a significant
role in Lorena’s progress. Participating
in the Chicago Summer Opera program allowed her to establish essential
connections and put herself out there.
She continues to maintain relationships with the people she met through
various programs, including a rehearsal pianist at Juilliard, with whom she
practiced almost every day. Furthermore, the relationships Lorena made at
Binghamton University have led her to
other professional relationships. One of
her former professors, Mary Burgess,
connected her with the teacher who
helped her perfect her technique for
graduate auditions.
When preparing for graduate school
applications, Lorena quickly realized
how important it is to be 100% confident in yourself. The most important
part is perfecting your technique, and
that happens at your own pace; there
is no need to rush the process.
For Lorena, audition season meant
practicing every single day. Even when
not practicing vocally, she focused on
mental preparation, stressing the importance of vocal rest.

title: Page 9
text:
—“It gets easier the more auditions you do,” she advised. “Never let fear hold you
back from pursuing something that can advance your career.”
Studying at the Jacobs School of Music has also provided Lorena with numerous networking opportunities. “They have lots of lunch networking events, kind
of like Harpur Edge,” she noted. Lorena also praised Harpur Edge for helping her
develop skills in networking and knowing how to promote herself effectively.
Jacobs’s large-scale program feels like a big step into the professional world for
Lorena. “I’ve always wanted to be in this competitive school, where all my classmates are just inspirations to me, where they speak all these languages and come
from all over the world. Our faculty, too, is just amazing.”
Lorena has also managed to expand her connections beyond the opera world. Through her English background, she connected with another Binghamton alumna and promoted one of
her published works. Lorena has learned to
take the skills she has learned from a variety
of experiences and implement them into
her career. “My ability to talk to people of
all different backgrounds has gone a long
way in making connections outside of music as well.”
Reflecting on her overall journey, she added,
“Everything has brought me to
this moment.”
When asked for advice for current music and
arts students, she emphasized how rejection
should be viewed as a learning experience.
“No means not right now—not never. In this
career, you cannot take rejection personally.
You need to brush it off and move on.”
Lorena’s story illustrates the power of determination, networking, and continuous
growth in the arts. As she refines her technique and navigates the competitive landscape at the Jacobs School of Music, her
commitment to opera continues to drive
her forward.
-Grace Scollo, Harpur Edge

title: Page 13
text:
PIP, our Harpur Edge Hedgehog,
is always ready to level up —
Professionally, Intellectually, and
Personally — but what should they
wear? That’s up to you!
Cut out and mix and match fun
accessories, outfits, and props to
dress PIP for any creative occasion. A
paint-splattered hat and brush for a
day in the studio? A microphone for
the big stage? A film camera for the
next big movie? Maybe a
theater mask for a starring role? The
possibilities are endless!
Let your imagination run wild and give
PIP a look that’s as bold and creative
as the School of the Arts itself!
Do Not Cut
Glue face on

on line
your way!
